coredata.dc:title: Dual roles of a transparent polymer film containing dispersed N-doped carbon dots: A high-efficiency blue light converter and UV screen
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar Barman, Barun
Nagao, Tadaaki
Nanda, Karuna Kar
coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis of a transparent plastic material with very high performance ultraviolet (UV)-blocking and blue-light (440 nm) emission. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was employed as a transparent plastic matrix that disperses N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) prepared via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, ethylene diamine, and HCl solution. Luminescence of these N-CDs is excitation-independent and the quantum yield (QY) is maximal at an excitation wavelength range of 350–370 nm, in the UV-A radiation segment of the solar spectrum. By encapsulating N-CDs in a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, the absolute QY achieves 91%, which is higher than in aqueous solution. As the particle concentration increases in PVA matrix or in solution, UV absorbance increases and QY decreases. On the other hand, UV absorbance of the film is proportional to thickness with no appreciable deterioration in QY, a quality that is beneficial for UV light absorption as well as conversion into blue light. We propose that this N-CD/PVA composite is an ideal transparent plastic for UV-ray shielding, as well as being useful for blue light emission, composed only of eco-friendly nontoxic elements. The high UV absorption and strong luminescence of N-CDs are beneficial also for other applications that include anti-counterfeiting (secret inks) and greenhouse sheathing that simultaneously blocks UV-rays while generating blue light to promote plant growth.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
High quantum yield
Solid state luminescence
UV screen
Polyvinyl alcohol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescence, UV-absorbance, and transmittance of N-CDs/PVA film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM and HRTEM images of N-CDs. (c) AFM image of N-CDs. (d-f) XPS survey, C 1s and N 1s spectra. (g and h) Raman and FTIR spectra of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of N-CDs in aqueous solution. (b) Photographs of transparent N-CD/PVA films (2.5 μL/mL) (I and II), and a high N-CD-concentration PVA film (50 μL/mL) (III). (c) Photograph of two films under UV illumination at 365 nm. (d and e) N-CDs in PVA under visible and UV light. (f) Schematic illustration of low and high concentrations of N-CDs in PVA matrices. (g) Transmittance spectra of various concentrations of N-CDs in PVA matrices on quartz substrates.

(a) UV-absorbance spectra of different concentrations of N-CDs in PVA solution. (b) Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of N-CDs in solid N-CD/PVA films. (c) Fluorescence of different concentrations of N-CDs in solution. (d) QYs of different concentrations of N-CDs in N-CD/PVA films on a quartz substrate. Experiments were performed with an excitation at 365 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of a solid film of N-CDs in PVA matrix. (b) Comparison of fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in solution and in a PVA matrix in a solid film. The excitation wavelength is 365 nm. (c) Absorbance spectra and (d) QY as a function of thickness of the N-CD/PVA film. (e and f) display the cross sectional SEM of 1st and 2nd layer of an N-CD/PVA film. (g and h) AFM image of the upper surface of the N-CD/PVA film.
Layer-by-layer formation of different concentrations of N-CD/PVA matrices on quartz substrates in (a) daylight and (b) UV light (365 nm).

Photographs of sprouts grown in water containing N-CDs, illuminated with (a) visible and (b) UV light, respectively. Photographs of a two 1000-yen notes. One is marked with N-CD ink and the other is unmarked, under (c) visible and (d) UV light, respectively.

(a) Transmittance spectra of N-CD/PVA films. The thickness of one layer is 40 μm. (b) Schematic illustration of N-CD/PVA utilized as a UV-blocking and visible-transparent greenhouse material. Inset photograph shows a typical greenhouse on which our film can be used. (c) Schematic demonstration of UV-A blocking by N-CD/PVA film. (d) Demonstration of UV-blocking performance: (I) N-CDs solution directly on top of a UV-LED torch; (II) A single-layer of N-CD/PVA film (on a glass slide) inserted between the N-CD solution and the UV-LED torch; (III) A double-layer of N-CD/PVA film (on a glass slide) inserted between the N-CD solution and the UV-LED torch. A double-layer N-CD/PVA film (75 μm) completely absorbs the UV light.

Schematic illustration of conversion of UV light into blue light using an N-CD-containing PVA (N-CD/PVA) cap as a UV-to-blue light converter. They are placed (upper right) on a UV torch (UV LED pen) and (lower right) on the bare LED device, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

row.entry: CA (1.5 g) + 0.5 mL EDA + 1 g Urea
CA (1.5 g) + 1.5 mL EDA + 0.25 mL HCl acid (11.6 M) (pH: ~11)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mouse masses during and after acute toxicity testing. Body mass is expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (g). Numbers of animals are shown in parentheses.

head.ce:title: Dual roles of a transparent polymer film containing dispersed N-doped carbon dots: A high-efficiency blue light converter and UV screen

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Condensed Matter Physics Graduate School of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Condensed Matter Physics Graduate School of Science, Hokkaido University, Kita-10 Nishi-8 Kita-ku, Sapporo 060-0810, Japan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: International Center for Materials Nanoarchitectonics (MANA), National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS), Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305-0044, Japan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis of a transparent plastic material with very high performance ultraviolet (UV)-blocking and blue-light (440 nm) emission. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was employed as a transparent plastic matrix that disperses N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) prepared via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, ethylene diamine, and HCl solution. Luminescence of these N-CDs is excitation-independent and the quantum yield (QY) is maximal at an excitation wavelength range of 350–370 nm, in the UV-A radiation segment of the solar spectrum. By encapsulating N-CDs in a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, the absolute QY achieves 91%, which is higher than in aqueous solution. As the particle concentration increases in PVA matrix or in solution, UV absorbance increases and QY decreases. On the other hand, UV absorbance of the film is proportional to thickness with no appreciable deterioration in QY, a quality that is beneficial for UV light absorption as well as conversion into blue light. We propose that this N-CD/PVA composite is an ideal transparent plastic for UV-ray shielding, as well as being useful for blue light emission, composed only of eco-friendly nontoxic elements. The high UV absorption and strong luminescence of N-CDs are beneficial also for other applications that include anti-counterfeiting (secret inks) and greenhouse sheathing that simultaneously blocks UV-rays while generating blue light to promote plant growth.

ce:section.ce:para: Absorption and re-emission of light, especially absorption of ultraviolet (UV) light and re-emission of blue light, have potentially great applications in solar cells and in greenhouse UV shielding, since they promote incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) in Si photovoltaics and photosynthesis [1,2]. Due to the recent rapid spread of low-cost GaN-based UV light-emitting devices, several strategies such as color mixing and color conversion have been adopted, and blue emission is an integral part of white light emission [3–7]. Though blue light-emitting diodes with yellow phosphors have already been commercialized, researchers are keen to realize ultraviolet or infra-red light emitting diodes for white light emission as well [8–11]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn increasing attention in various research fields due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, stable emission, and great potential to replace costly and toxic semiconductor quantum dots (CdS, CdSe, CdTe, etc.) and rare earth-doped oxynitride-based phosphors that contain toxic heavy metals [12–31].

However, CD florescence deteriorates in solid form due to self-quenching by CD aggregation [32]. Though most applications have employed colloidal suspensions of CDs and have been studied extensively, there is great demand for solid state CD luminescence in industrial applications. For example, several research groups have attempted to use CDs as an active material in light-emitting devices (LEDs) by incorporating them into various polymer matrices [33–45]. Furthermore, since CDs degrade in air, they must be encapsulated in a suitable matrix. Therefore, such matrices should not only disperse the CDs to prevent solid-state quenching, but also to serve as a mechanical support and to provide protection against atmospheric oxidation. Such matrices must be flexible, readily scalable, eco-friendly, stable, and mechanically robust. Above all, they need to be highly transparent in the UV/VIS region. If CDs can be encapsulated and dispersed in a UV–VIS transparent polymer matrix, oxidation and solid-state quenching are expected to be well suppressed. Recently, some research groups have been using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a polymer matrix for blue light-emitting applications [46–49]. Nonetheless, their florescence properties differ from solution to solid state. The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs depends drastically on the number of CDs in the polymer matrix. Most CDs show high QY at minute concentrations, but QY decreases drastically with increasing CD concentration due to aggregation-induced luminescence quenching in both solution and solid state [50]. Thus far, no studies of CDs have demonstrated excellent transparency and high QY in the solid state. Accordingly, it is essential to study polymer-embedded CDs to achieve both.

There is increasing interest in preventing overexposure of human skin and eyes to UV light, because of its adverse impact on human health [51]. There are three main types of UV light: UV-C (100 nm-280 nm), UV-B (280 nm-315 nm) and UV-A (315 nm-400 nm). On the earth’s surface, UV-A and UV-B rays are transmitted through the atmosphere, while UV-C and part of the UV-B spectrum are absorbed by the Earth’s ozone layer. Transmitted UV-A and UV-B rays cause serious damage to skin and eyes, leading to premature aging and skin cancer. With their longer wavelengths that can penetrate to the middle layer of human skin (dermis), UV-A rays are especially dangerous. UV light also photodegrades polymeric materials by activating chromophores [52]. Most UV-blocking polymeric substrates are fabricated by the addition of organic dyes which can be degraded by UV light. On the other hand, most inorganic UV absorbers suffer from either low transmittance in the visible wavelengths or UV-blocking properties [51–53]. Therefore, it is essential to develop UV-absorbing or blocking materials with high environmental- and bio-compatibility, that are completely transparent to visible light.

Herein, we demonstrate two major applications of the same N-doped carbon dot (N-CD)/PVA matrix. (1) It is a highly luminescent emitter of blue light with an ~91% QY from UV-LED. (2) It is a complete UV-A blocker (Scheme 1 ) that is highly transparent to visible light. Thus these N-CDs play a dual function.

N-CDs are prepared via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA) solution. Luminescence of these N-CDs is excitation-independent and their quantum yield (QY) is optimal at an excitation wavelength range corresponding to that of UV-A radiation (360–370 nm). The absolute QY is as high as 91% for N-CDs embedded in PVA-matrix films, which is higher than that of N-CDs in solution. When the particle concentration is increased, UV absorbance increases with a slight decrease in QY, both in the PVA matrix as well as in solution. On the other hand, absorbance of the films is proportional to their thickness, with no appreciable deterioration in QY, which is highly beneficial for UV shielding and wavelength conversion. Our overall conclusion is that N-CDs in PVA matrices can provide highly-transparent, versatile UV-shielding with high environmental/bio-compatibility, while this material also has strong potential to replace widely used, rare earth-doped oxinitrides and semiconductor quantum dot-based phosphors. In this regards, these N-CDs or N-CDs/PVA films offer perfectly effective shielding materials to block dangerous UV radiation.

N-CDs can easily be synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA). Here, we combined 0.25 mL of 11.6 M HCl, 1.5 g of CA, and 1.5 mL of EDA solution in 20 mL of H2O. Addition of HCl improves the QY. In brief, 20 mL of solution were prepared and placed in a 50-mL autoclave. The whole assembly was placed in a low-temperature incubator and treated at different temperatures for different durations (Table 1 ). After synthesis, the colorless solution became dark brown, indicating N-CD formation. After synthesis, N-CDs were mixed with an excess volume of ethanol solution (1:3 ratio) and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles. After centrifugation, the solution was dried in a vacuum oven and then re-dispersed in 20 mL deionized water for further characterization and application.

Purified samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In order to prepare N-CDs embedded in a PVA matrix, PVA solution was prepared by adding 2 g of PVA powder to 60 mL of de-ionized (DI) water and kept at 90 °C for 3 h. Different amounts of N-CD solution were mixed with 4 mL of PVA solution. Then 0.5 mL of PVA/N-CD solution were drop-cast on a quartz substrate and dried at 90 °C to obtain a solid film. Absorbance and fluorescence spectra of both the solution and solid film were measured using a JASCO (V-570) UV–VIS-NIR spectrometer and a JASCO (FP8500) spectrometer. The absolute quantum yield (QY) of the N-CDs, both in solution and in a solid state were measured using a Hamamatsu C9920-02G integrating sphere system, coupled with a 150 W xenon lamp and a PMA-12 photonic multichannel analyzer. For the solution, at first we used a 1-cm path-length quartz cuvette with deionized water as a reference sample, followed by different concentrations of N-CD solution. For measurement of the solid sample, we used a quartz substrate as a reference in the dish-sized sample holder in the instrument, followed by measurement of the N-CD/PVA film.

TRPL spectroscopy was performed using a WItec instrument. Excitation was achieved with a 470-nm pulsed laser diode that was focused onto the samples with a microscope objective. A suitable long-pass edge filter was used to block excitation laser emission during PL emission to a spectrometer. Time-correlated, single-photon counting curves for time-resolved PL spectroscopy were acquired using a time correlator (Single Photon Avalanche Diodes (SPADs) from MPD micro-photon devices).

Fenugreek sprouts were grown in Millipore water along with N-CD solution. At first, Fenugreek seeds were cleaned in Millipore water and kept overnight with excess N-CDs in water. After that, seeds were removed and kept on a glass plate in a light/dark room for 2–4 days in ambient conditions and sprayed with N-CD aqueous solution every 8–10 h. Fenugreek sprouts were grown on the glass plate.

Invisible ink is well-exploited for forensic and counterfeiting cases. As synthesized, diluted N-CD aqueous solution was directly used as invisible ink on 1000 yen Japanese currency for counterfeiting demonstration.

An acute oral toxicity test of highly fluorescent N-CDs was performed using five-week female ICR mice purchased from Japan SLC Inc. Control and experimental groups each comprised 5 animals. Test animals were fasted for about 4 h before administration. After determining the mass of each animal, experimental animals received N-CD solution (0.125 mg/mL) and control animals received water, administered by gavage (20 µL/g) using a gastric tube. Individual masses were again determined 7 and 14 days after the test. No deaths were observed in either group during the observation period. No abnormalities were observed and no differences in mass were noted in the test group compared to the control group (Table 2 ). During necropsy at the end of the observation period, no abnormalities were observed in any test animals, suggesting that at this acute dose, they are non-toxic to mice.

We synthesized N-CDs hydrothermally and optimized the process (Table 1). N-CDs with the highest QY were further characterized in terms of UV-opacity and luminescence. Well-dispersed N-CD particles were clearly visible in transmission electron microscope (TEM) images (Fig. 1 a), which revealed an average particle size of ~7.0 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1b) showed a lattice fringe of 0.25 nm that corresponds to the (1120) planes of graphite. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging (Fig. 1c) also indicated a particle size comparable to that observed by TEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra confirmed that the N-CDs contain only C, N, and O (Fig. 1d). De-convoluted high-resolution XPS spectra displayed a C 1s peak at 284.6 eV, and a different type of N 1s peak was observed (Fig. 1e-f).

The N/C atomic ratio was ~40%, indicative of heavy N incorporation into the CDs. De-convoluted C 1s spectra included a CC, CC peak with a binding energy of ~284.6 eV, a CN/CN peak with binding energy 286 eV, a CO peak with a binding energy of ~287.6 eV, a carboxylic group (COOH) peak with a binding energy of ~289.1 eV, and a shake-up peak with a binding energy of ~291.2 eV. The shake-up peak is a characteristic of conjugated systems or aromatic groups, which indicate the presence of hexagonal graphene-like structure inside the CDs [54–59]. The high resolution spectrum of N 1 s confirms the dominance of pyridine-like structure at 397.75 eV, followed by pyrrolic-like structure at 399.13 eV, pyridinic N-oxide (~404 eV) and graphitic N (~402 eV) (Fig. 1f) [54–57,60]. The O/C ratio is estimated to be ~52%, indicating a large amount of N-CD surface functionalization. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1g) clearly shows a feature at 1563–1580 cm−1 (a G band), indicating the presence of CC bonds with sp2 nature, while the feature at 1360–1393 cm−1 (D band) indicates CC bonds with a character close to that of sp3 bonds or defects in the graphene framework. An FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs revealed the presence of CO, CN, NO, CC, OCO, CH and NH structural units (Fig. 1h), which reportedly lead to high optical performance [25–28,54–57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence, UV-absorbance, and transmittance of N-CDs/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: UV-absorption spectra of N-CDs in aqueous solution display two strong absorption peaks at 351 nm and 242 nm, corresponding to n to π* transition of CO and CN and π to π* transition of CC and CC bonds, respectively [54–58] (Fig. 2 a). Normalized excitation (PLE) and emission spectra reveal strong absorbance at 350 nm and photoluminescence at 441 nm, respectively. The strong blue luminescence in daylight demonstrates the high QY of N-CDs in solution (QY: 80%). A low concentration (2.5 μL/mL) N-CD/PVA film displays high transparency (Fig. 2b, I and II); however, a high concentration of N-CDs in PVA film (25 μL/mL) reduces the optical transparency of the film and causes it to develop a faint yellow color (Fig. 2b, III). Under 365-nm UV illumination, luminescence intensity decreases markedly with increasing N-CD concentration (Fig. 2c).

N-CD/PVA does not fluoresce under excitation with visible light, but does strongly with UV exposure (Fig. 2d and e). Even in daylight or under room light, blue luminescence is clearly observed, evidencing its strong emission intensity. Transmittance spectra reveal that N-CD/PVA matrices are very effective at absorbing UV light, especially UV-A radiation, while they are highly transparent in the visible region. At low N-CD concentrations, PVA films on quartz substrates transmit ~95% of visible light and absorption occurs only in the UV region (Fig. 2g). This indicates that the present N-CD/PVA system is a high-performance UV-to-blue-light converter. As such, this material would be well suited as UV-A-blocking coating for windows in building and automobiles, as well as for visible light transparent light emitting screens, sunglasses, UV-opaque optical filter films, etc.

UV-absorbance gradually increased with increasing concentrations of N-CDs, as shown by photographs and transmittance spectra (Fig. 3 a). With excitation at 365 nm, luminescence spectra of solid N-CDs/PVA film on quartz substrates at various concentrations are excitation-independent, similar to the results obtained for N-CDs in solution (Fig. 3b; Fig. S1). As with N-CDs in solution, fluorescence intensity initially increases and then decreases with increasing N-CD concentration (Fig. 3c). This is a general rule, due to self-quenching. At very high concentrations, the interparticle distance decreases so as to enhance interactions (and mutual reabsorption) between particles, resulting in decreased fluorescence intensity [24,49]. Based on the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3b), QY is expected to follow the trend of fluorescence intensity. In contrast to this expectation, the QY simply decreases monotonically with increased N-CD concentration in PVA film (Figs. 2g and 3d). To enable practical applications, it is essential to determine the optimal concentration for both QY and transparency. Luminescence is weak for a 0.025 μL/mL solution compared to that of a 2.5 μL/mL solution, but the QY of the former is 91% while that of the latter is only 77% (Fig. 3d). The higher value of QY at a lower concentration is due to weaker interactions among dots, as well as lower self-absorbance. Significantly, QY is only 0.06% for an N-CD powder on a quartz substrate.

Fluorescence spectra of solid films containing N-CDs in a PVA matrix are also independent of the excitation wavelength, as are N-CDs in PVA solution (Fig. 4 a, b). Luminescence spectra are slightly blue-shifted from 442 nm in solution to 432 nm in the solid film PVA matrix. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report that compares spectra and QY of quantum dots in solution with those in a solid thin film, while simultaneously studying their transparency. Unexpectedly, the QY was as high as 91%, higher than the 84% of the PVA solution. This is the highest QY achieved in solid state so far. We note that the QY can be enhanced by two means: by increasing the N-doping [24] and/or by reducing the size of N-CDs by enclosing them in a polymer matrix [16]. Since the N-doping does not change when the N-CDs are embedded tightly in matrix, compression by the PVA matrix probably results in N-CD shrinkage [16] causing a blue-shift in fluorescence spectra, as well as an enhancement in QY.

The enhancement in QY and the blue shift in PL spectra can be further analyzed by time-resolved photo-luminance (TRPL) spectra, both in solution and in a PVA polymer matrix. Due to the lack of an instrumental facility, we excited the solution and film with a 470-nm pulsed laser diode. TRPL spectra were fitted using a bi-exponential decay function (Fig. S2). The average lifetime of N-CDs embedded in the polymer (τavg) was ~10.72 ns and 1.54 ns in solution. These longer τavg and stretched decay profile in the solid film indicate that non-radiative (NR) defect sites are suppressed by the polymer. PVA polymers can form rigid hydrogen bonds between polymer OH groups and oxygen-containing functional groups in the N-CDs, leading to suppression of NR active centers [45,46]. When NR defects are reduced, the ratio of emission from the core (higher emission energy) and surface state (lower emission energy) is enhanced, leading to a blue shift in the PL spectrum with a higher QY. As the N-CD concentration increases, absorbance continues to increase, but the QY decreases due to self-quenching. However, both high absorbance and high quantum yield are essential for practical applications.

In an attempt to enhance absorbance, while avoiding deterioration of the QY and visible light transparency, we increased the thickness of the film by adding another layer. As expected, the UV absorbance increased, following a linear relationship with thickness (Fig. 4c), while the QY decreased by only a few percent (Fig. 4d-f). Thicknesses of the two films were ~40 μm and 75 μm. Both ESI and AFM images reveal that the surface of the transparent N-CD/PVA film is very smooth (Fig. 4g-h), with average roughness of ~3–6 nm.

We tested N-CD/PVA films of different thicknesses with various concentrations under daylight and UV light illumination (Fig. 5 a-b). At high N-CD concentrations in PVA film, N-CDs aggregated, resulting in a loss of transparency and luminescence, due to self-absorption by N-CDs in the PVA film. High N-CD concentrations cause the film transparent to develop a yellow color, which is also evident from its transmittance spectrum (Fig. 2g) and from its gross appearance (Figs. 2b:III and 5a). Thus, the best way to maintain both transparency and luminescence is to employ a low concentration of N-CDs in PVA matrices and to increase the film thickness layer-by-layer. Overall, our results encourage the use of N-CDs in PVA matrices for conversion of UV to blue light.

Chemical stability of the N-CD/PVA film is critical for long-term applications. N-CDs in PVA film are stable in atmospheric conditions and the QY remains unchanged after several days with no change in the PL spectrum (Fig. S3).

In order to further explore applications of N-CDs, we grew Fenugreek sprouts using water containing N-CDs. Photographs of the sprouts were taken under visible and UV light (365 nm) (Fig. 6 a-b). Interestingly, the signature blue light emission of N-CDs can be seen in the sprouts grown in water with N-CDs, clearly demonstrating their acute bio-compatibility. In terms of anti-counterfeiting applications, we dried N-CD aqueous solution on the currency. Dried N-CDs show very strong luminescence when exposed to UV light, which can be seen by the unaided eye.

Most N-CD-based anti-counterfeiting agents require ~10% PVA solution or must be wetted to activate luminescence [61–63]. We have studied anti-counterfeiting applications of N-CDs. When one of two 1000-yen notes was marked with the acronym “NIMS”, the two notes were indistinguishable under visible light, but in UV light the acronym was clearly visible (Fig. 6c-d).

Another important application of this N-CD/PVA film is as an efficient UV shield with excellent visible light transparency. Transmittance spectra for one and two layers of N-CD/PVA film fabricated with an N-CD concentration of 12.5 μL/mL, clearly show that a single layer of N-CD/PVA film (~40 μm) absorbs most of the harmful UV-A light, while transmitting ~94% of visible light component (Fig. 7 a). However, complete blocking of UV-A was not possible with only a single 40-µm layer of this film. If we increase the N-CD concentration to 50 μL/mL in PVA, UV-A is completely blocked, but the film loses its visible light transparency (~75%) (Fig. 2g). To overcome this drawback, so as to achieve high transparency with complete UV-A blocking, another layer of N-CDs/PVA film (~40 μm) was coated on top of the first layer. This two-layer N-CD/PVA film exhibited visible light transparency similar to that of the single-layer film, but it blocked UV-A light completely (Fig. 7a). This remarkable property makes our N-CD/PVA film highly compatible with practical applications requiring low-cost, bio-compatible, transparent, UV protecting/blocking plastic.

One important application could be for greenhouse crop cultivation systems (Fig. 7b). Blocking UV light in greenhouses has important advantages, such as protecting plants from hazardous UV light, while transmitting photosynthetically useful visible radiation for plant growth [64]. Blocking UV-A also affords protection from insect pests, such as whiteflies, aphids, and thrips, which are guided by UV-A to the crops they destroy [65]. Thus, complete UV-A blocking with N-CD/PVA could be highly beneficial for greenhouses.

As already stated, N-CDs are very efficient at converting UV to blue light. When an N-CD solution is placed directly on top of a UV LED torch, strong blue luminescence from the N-CD solution is evident (Fig. 7c; 7d: I). However, when an N-CD/PVA film is inserted between the N-CD solution and the UV torch (Fig. 7d: II), only very weak blue luminescence results. With a second N-CD/PVA film, the remaining UV light is completely absorbed and there is no luminescence from the N-CD solution (Fig. 7d: III). This simple experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of the UV blocking activity of this N-CD/PVA film, which has many potential applications involving UV protection.

These N-CDs can also be used as fluorescent biomarkers in applications in which a variety of expensive and toxic synthetic dyes are currently used. Replacing them with low-cost fluorescent materials made of nontoxic materials is necessary for biomedical applications, and our N-CDs are well-suited to such purposes, as demonstrated by an acute toxicity test, based on the OECD Guidelines for Testing of Chemicals 420 (2001). Female mice were injected with these highly fluorescent N-CDs. No discernible effect was detected in mouse organs. The N-CDs were non-toxic to mice at all doses. Thus, they have strong potential for in vivo fluorescence imaging, such as for tumor or iPS cell tracking, together with numerous potential uses in commodities, such as secret inks, food container, drink bottles, etc.

In conclusion, we propose a novel transparent plastic material for high-performance UV-A shielding and blue light emission based on nontoxic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as wavelength converters. These N-CDs are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylene diamine solution. Luminescence of these N-CDs is excitation-independent and both absorbance and quantum yield are optimal at an excitation wavelength of 360–370 nm. The quantum yield is as high as 91% for N-CDs embedded in PVA matrix films, which exceeds that in solution. When the particle concentration is increased, absorbance increases with decreasing quantum yield. On the other hand, absorbance of the films is proportional to thickness with negligible deterioration of quantum yield, which is very beneficial for light-converting materials. Because this N-CD/PVA film is a very efficient UV absorber with high visible transparency (~90%), it can be used as a powerful, low-cost, flexible, UV shield/blocker, leading to wider applications of such materials. Its excellent bio-compatibility, demonstrated by in vivo toxicity testing, shows that these N-CDs appear safe and may have significant applications in bio-imaging/sensing and drug delivery, as well as applications in agricultural and daily commodities.

In this study, T. Nagao has initiated and led the project, K.K. Nanda and B.K. Barman has started the synthesis of sample in Nagao’s lab. Then Barman and Nanda have done the characterization, analyzed the data, and all has contributed to the discussions and wrote the manuscript. This study, T. Nagao has initiated and led the project, K.K. Nanda and B.K. Barman has started the synthesis of sample in Nagao’s lab. Then Barman and Nanda have done the characterization, analyzed the data, and all has contributed to the discussions and wrote the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: BKB and KKN acknowledge NIMS, Tsukuba for use of its facilities and for support via student internship and short-term visiting scientist programs. The authors also acknowledge helpful technical support from K. Okano and helpful discussions with Dr. Satish Laxman Shinde. This work is also supported by the JST CREST program (grant number JPMJCR13C3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.145405.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
